posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
One of the most surprising things I heard in the recent IDTT podcast was Morris’ claim that many bottles from 1996 white Burgundy (from his cellar) are turning out to be good aged white burgs, tasting as expected. These are the same bottlings/crus that when opened years earlier were horribly premoxed. He surmised that there was some character of premature oxidation such that bottles, if kept long enough, could get past. In effect, they’d become nicely aged, and no longer oxidized.

Admittedly he was uncertain of how or why this could happen. And he couldn’t prove that good bottles were previously premoxed. Still it’s intriguing. Has anyone seen this? Can anyone hypothesize how or why this might happen? Is there hope for long aging white burg?

link_counts.title: 2019/2020 Premox? Say it ain't so! (or Heat Damage?)
2019/2020 Premox? Say it ain't so! (or Heat Damage?)

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: How can he prove the bottle was premoxed before?

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: I’ve heard him say this before, which is non-sensical.

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked: Digressing, thought his discussion on using clonal selection made bad pinot was interesting. Bad news for Oregon if true.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Isn’t this what supposedly happened to the judgment of Paris wine?

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
I’ve heard him say this before, which is non-sensical.

This. It makes no sense. Literally.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
A couple of points here.

Perhaps he was talking about bottles that he had seen or had himself that showed a deeper darker color and we thought to be bad based on that color alone? That would explain what he’s talking about.

Secondly, we still know so little about the problem that I wouldn’t rule anything out in terms of what may or may not happen moving forward with these wines. Yes, it doesn’t make much sense, but that’s one of the amazing things to me about wine. It doesn’t always make sense

And as far as clonal selections vs field or masale, many have been saying the same thing based on certain sites in Burgundy. I think it is simply too early to make that statement at this point. We simply don’t know enough.

Cheers

posts.name: Jeff Vaughan
posts.cooked: If I remember correctly, I think he said something like he had opened some bottles from the same case or batch that were all premoxed. He quit opening them for some period of time and then when he started opening them again, they showed well. Still, no way of knowing if the latter bottles were fine all along.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: What was the deal with the 73 montelena where they were dark but then cleared up?

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: what is non-sensical about the argument? I think it’s a little too convenient (if you open a premoxed bottle it’s now your fault for not waiting long enough for the issue to resolve) but is there some reason why what Jasper is saying - which is that there is a temporary condition that mirrors oxidation and then the wine returns to normal - is necessarily untrue?

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked: Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
How can he prove the bottle was premoxed before?

+1. Some bottles in a case are premoxed and some aren’t. If one could reliably tell which-without opening the bottle—that would be huge.

posts.name: Chris Buchanan
posts.cooked:
This is a fascinating idea. First I have no experience with this particular wine and I haven’t listened to the podcast.

However, I can see an argument for early negative qualities (in this case pre-mox) resolving over time. The chemical reactions in wine, particularly in wine aging are quite complex and are not completely understood.

I’ve seen negative traits in young wines resolve in the cellar (I’ve also seen them get much worse). This is sometimes hard to predict unless you have specific experience with a wine. For example I’ve seen certain wines that often have an acetone (nail polish) hint at pressing, but by the end of 6-9 months in barrel it is no longer a prominent or offensive trait.

I can imagine two ways (perhaps more) that an early flaw could resolve:

  1. the underlying wine is sound just “out of balance” and at the point of noticing the premox the other attributes are wonky and the prominent feature is the oxidation. As the wine matures the other characteristics mature and bring it in balance so that you have a nicely aged wine. Basically reactions occur at different rates. Like a teenager with disjointed proportions, you just have to wait until the wine grows into its body.

  2. the underlying wine is sound and the premox is just a step in the aging process for this particularl wine. Kind of like the “dumb” period in longer aged wines (the window of mediocrity between the initial fruit and the ultimate aged complexity). So basically whatever compounds are causing the premox are then slowly oxidized further or combine with other oxidized/reduced compounds to produce an ultimate compound which is pleasant and characteristic of a finely aged Burgundy.

This isn’t to say all premox wine is ultimately going to be great (or even good), but that it is perhaps possible to have a funky step in the process of full evolution of a finer wine.

I’ll have to check out the podcast, cool concept!

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

How in the world is that evidence against the theory?

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
what is non-sensical about the argument? I think it’s a little too convenient (if you open a premoxed bottle it’s now your fault for not waiting long enough for the issue to resolve) but is there some reason why what Jasper is saying - which is that there is a temporary condition that mirrors oxidation and then the wine returns to normal - is necessarily untrue?

Not a chemist but I seriously doubt that the reactions leading to premox are reversible. I would need more evidence than one guy’s opinion to be convinced otherwise, no matter how respected he might be.

My guess is that the good bottles were simply the survivors in the case that were never premoxed in the first place.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
what is non-sensical about the argument? I think it’s a little too convenient (if you open a premoxed bottle it’s now your fault for not waiting long enough for the issue to resolve) but is there some reason why what Jasper is saying - which is that there is a temporary condition that mirrors oxidation and then the wine returns to normal - is necessarily untrue?

Not a chemist but I seriously doubt that the reactions leading to premox are reversible. I would need more evidence than one guy’s opinion to be convinced otherwise, no matter how respected he might be.

My guess is that the good bottles were simply the survivors in the case that were never premoxed in the first place.

I doubt it too but Morris is no dummy. And again, the idea is that the wines aren’t actually oxidized and that the effect is later reversed, but suffering from something that temporarily replicates oxidation. I believe he points to Rhône whites as going through a similar phase, but I can’t put my hands on the quote. It strikes me that whatever is happening is complicated. Complicated enough that some pretty smart minds can’t figure it out. If it is true that folks are now opening 1996 Bonneau du martray or leflaive in quantity and finding those wines to drink as one would have expected notwithstanding premox, I would find that really interesting.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Before everyone goes off saying Morris (no relation) is crazy, read his article in The World of Fine Wine in 2014. It’s a very thoughtful piece. A few key portions:

As a wine professional, I am pretty sure I can tell if something is oxidized: the color, the bouquet, the taste on the palate. But what if I am wrong? > What if there is another effect that shows the same symptoms but is in fact a different process? > I wouldn’t even entertain the notion if it were not for one factor: On a number of occasions I have experienced wines that appeared to be oxidized that have subsequently returned to the fold…

I can only infer one of two explanations: > Either oxidation is reversible, or else the symptoms of oxidation that seem compelling are in fact something else. > Those with a better grounding in chemistry than I suggest that the conversion of ethanol to acetaldehyde is not reversible-but perhaps the oxidation of flavonoids can be. Certainly all winemakers have experience of detecting some oxidation in barrel or tank and reversing it with a judicious dose of sulfur.

It should also be noted that (a) some wines that have oxidized in a normal manner have been described as premoxed, and (b) such is the fear of this issue currently that many wines are immediately accused of the pox if there is the faintest hint of oxidation (or sometimes other deviations), when time in the glass might deliver a fine and untainted wine. [Steve] Tanzer was taken to task for suggesting something like this back in 2008, but he may well have been right.

link_counts.title: What causes White Burgundy to oxidize? - World Of Fine Wine

posts.cooked:
If it is true that folks are now opening 1996 Bonneau du martray or leflaive in quantity and finding those wines to drink as one would have expected notwithstanding premox, I would find that really interesting.

posts.name: Fred C

Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Yeah, we’ve heard this before. Certainly, in my experience, there are some few wines where an early verdict would have been “premoxed” but they actually weren’t - I’m not really up on wine chemistry, but I suspect those bottles are / were actually highly reduced. And then when you drink them now they’re actually good. But that is a very small portion of wines and “premoxed” ones. I’ve had this happen for example with '96 Ramonet. There’s then a small number of other wines that showed early signs of oxidation, but some bottles were “enjoyable” many years later - e.g. I saw this in some BdM CC 96, which at age 20 was a bit more oxidative than I’d want but not a million miles from what would be forgivable in a 20 yr old wine - somehow, the early oxidation just paused or progressed only quite slowly. And then there’s the majority, where you’d really only want to cling onto Jasper’s story if you’re going to auction !!

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: If premox is not actually oxidation and can be cleared up over time… then shouldn’t the vast majority clear up then with time or more oxygen instead of small random cases?

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

How in the world is that evidence against the theory?

Becasuse that 71 or 91 should be un oxidized by now? The theory is that if you hold it long enough then the oxidation goes away. Or do you think the theory is only for bottles 96 and after?

The theory is that the bottles aren’t oxidized but are going through a temporary phase that shows like oxidation. In any event, a ‘71 or ‘91 tells us nothing about premox. Any bottle of wine can oxidize, white burg or otherwise. We are talking about the particular phenomenon that started around ‘95/‘96.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
If it is true that folks are now opening 1996 Bonneau du martray or leflaive in quantity and finding those wines to drink as one would have expected notwithstanding premox, I would find that really interesting.

But where is the evidence of this???

Edit: the above quote does add some anecdotal evidence, but with the huge amount of experience on this board why has nobody here seen anything similar?

It’s just a theory. The evidence for it, according to Morris, is what he’s experiencing. I don’t think anyone on this board is saying it is a proven fact at this point. I don’t think Morris would say that. It’s just a hypothesis.

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked: A hypothesis without evidence? Pie in the sky?

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Before everyone goes off saying Morris (no relation) is crazy, read his article in The World of Fine Wine in 2014. It’s a very thoughtful piece. A few key portions:

As a wine professional, I am pretty sure I can tell if something is oxidized: the color, the bouquet, the taste on the palate. But what if I am wrong? > What if there is another effect that shows the same symptoms but is in fact a different process? > I wouldn’t even entertain the notion if it were not for one factor: On a number of occasions I have experienced wines that appeared to be oxidized that have subsequently returned to the fold…

I can only infer one of two explanations: > Either oxidation is reversible, or else the symptoms of oxidation that seem compelling are in fact something else. > Those with a better grounding in chemistry than I suggest that the conversion of ethanol to acetaldehyde is not reversible-but perhaps the oxidation of flavonoids can be. Certainly all winemakers have experience of detecting some oxidation in barrel or tank and reversing it with a judicious dose of sulfur.

It should also be noted that (a) some wines that have oxidized in a normal manner have been described as premoxed, and (b) such is the fear of this issue currently that many wines are immediately accused of the pox if there is the faintest hint of oxidation (or sometimes other deviations), when time in the glass might deliver a fine and untainted wine. [Steve] Tanzer was taken to task for suggesting something like this back in 2008, but he may well have been right.

We should careful not to confuse two different issues here. The issue of wines that seem off initially and might be felt to be oxidized but a few hours later come around is totally different from the concept that wines might be oxidized at a certain point in their life but come around a few years later.

It would be nice to be able to pin him down on what he is actually referring to by “coming back into the fold”, but in listening to his podcast, it appeared to me he was talking about wines coming back to being normal over the course of years. I have no idea how he felt that he would know that a wine would be previously oxidized, then back to normal, without sampling the wine at each phase, which as far as I know would be relatively impossible. (If he has actually done this, it would be nice to know how he accomplished it.).

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
A hypothesis without evidence? Pie in the sky?

The basis for the theory is his own tastings. You can call it insufficient if you want. 20 years out and there is no explanation for premox. I’m interested in what jasper has to say.

It should also be noted that (a) some wines that have oxidized in a normal manner have been described as premoxed, and (b) such is the fear of this issue currently that many wines are immediately accused of the pox if there is the faintest hint of oxidation (or sometimes other deviations), when time in the glass might deliver a fine and untainted wine. [Steve] Tanzer was taken to task for suggesting something like this back in 2008, but he may well have been right.

We should careful not to confuse two different issues here. The issue of wines that seem off initially and might be felt to be oxidized but a few hours later come around is totally different from the concept that wines might be oxidized at a certain point in their life but come around a few years later.

It would be nice to be able to pin him down on what he is actually referring to by “coming back into the fold”, but in listening to his podcast, it appeared to me he was talking about wines coming back to being normal over the course of years. I have no idea how he felt that he would know that a wine would be previously oxidized, then back to normal, without sampling the wine at each phase, which as far as I know would be relatively impossible. (If he has actually done this, it would be nice to know he accomplished it.).

I think what he is saying is that he has a number of bottles of wine X. Over a period of years, some/most/all of the bottles were oxidized. But recently, none have been. The number of bottles at issue is significant enough that he believes it isn’t mere happenstance.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

You are missing the point. Jasper’s contention is that the wines aren’t oxidized but are temporarily undergoing something that mimics oxidation.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked: Somebody asked a question about Ch Montelena. The answer is ‘pinking’…White Wine that has never been exposed to oxygen, as whites were made back then, can briefly turn color after bottling, when the wine is exposed to oxygen. Nowadays, white wines are made differently. But back then wine was protected at every stage with lots of SO2.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked: Sounds like cold fusion. . .

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

You are missing the point. Jasper’s contention is that the wines aren’t oxidized but are temporarily undergoing something that mimics oxidation.

Exactly.

Kris, here’s what he says near the end (my emphasis):

Some versions of the phenomenon appear to be temporary. Is it the apparent reversal of oxidation - which may perhaps be possible if the oxidation process has not gone too far - or the reversal of > apparent oxidation, if we are sometimes mistaking other complex processes for oxidation? > I am far from certain of either of these things, though they might mean that the total incidence of oxidized wines may not be quite as high as we fear.

Here are so, me specific examples he cites of the reversal of apparent oxidation:

The most astonishing case in my experience came during a tasting of several vintages of Domaine Bonneau du Martray’s Corton-Charlemagne. The 1996 was a dark brown on opening and smelled as though it were almost completely oxidized. I fancied there might still be a vestige of fruit on the otherwise oxidized palate, which was a surprise given the dark brown color. But wait… The color after just a few minutes of aeration was now a much paler brown-and 20 minutes later this was the best wine of the evening. A neighbor has experienced something very similar with some 2000 Chevalier-Montrachet from Colin Deléger.

I can only infer one of two explanations: Either oxidation is reversible, or else the symptoms of oxidation that seem compelling are in fact something else. Those with a better grounding in chemistry than I suggest that the conversion of ethanol to acetaldehyde is not reversible-but perhaps the oxidation of flavonoids can be. Certainly all winemakers have experience of detecting some oxidation in barrel or tank and reversing it with a judicious dose of sulfur.

In the article, he’s interested in this seemingly faux oxidation, and doesn’t claim that genuine oxidation is reversible.

posts.cooked:

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

You are missing the point. Jasper’s contention is that the wines aren’t oxidized but are temporarily undergoing something that mimics oxidation.

Which goes to my point. If that’s what it is, then a vast majority should get better, not just a small handful.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
We seem to want to explain all wine things by pure unequivocal science - sorry, folks - not gonna happen.

I’m sure we’ve all had wines that either should have been dead or appeared so upon opening and were drop dead gorgeous - or bottles that should have been awesome that were simply bleh . . .

These are the things that keep me going . . .

Cheers.

posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

Which goes to my point. If that’s what it is, then a vast majority should get better, not just a small handful.

I not sure I understand what your point is. I don’t see where Morris has said that only a small portion of the bottles get better. His theory is that the issue, whatever it is, goes away over time. In other words, the problem is that we’ve all been too impatient. And that what we call premature oxidation isn’t oxidation at all.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: If it’s not oxidation, why has adding sulfur (an anti-oxidant) fixed the issue in some domaines? And why have Diam and screwcaps that prevent oxidation prevented the problem? I call bullshit on his theory.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
If it’s not oxidation, why has adding sulfur (an anti-oxidant) fixed the issue in some domaines? And why have Diam and screwcaps that prevent oxidation prevented the problem? I call bullshit on his theory.

I don’t know any domain where adding sulfur has “fixed” the issue.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
I think Jasper is focusing on the faux oxidation phenomenon that most any of us have experienced who drink white burgs. this was a major topic of discussion 4-5 years ago when tanzer was bringing it up. But it becomes quite obvious after 1/2-1 hour of air which way a wine is going to go, and if you focus intensely on the wine, it doesn’t really have the hallmarks of true oxidation.

So for a while we all dutifully would leave our oxidized bottles to get several hours of air, even overnight, to be sure we we’re mis-identifiying them. But over time we learned that these bottles exist, but at least for me constitute perhaps constitute at most 5% of the seemingly oxidized bottles. the rest are just pre-moxed and get worse and worse with air.

For me this is an outdated and old topic that most of us have moved beyond. I’m surprised that Jasper is still stuck on it. (I wonder if he is drinking more pristine bottles in terms of provenance and that his curve of the phenomenon that we know of is delayed by several years).

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

Fermentation is a reductive process, as is malolactic, that quite literally “un-oxidizes” juice. I intentionally oxidize the white juice for our wines every year and during fermentation they move reductively back to a very fresh state.

That’s not really what this thread is relating too, but oxidation can be undone. It does not really leave you a “new” apple, just a fresh one.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I think Jasper is focusing on the faux oxidation phenomenon that most any of us have experienced who drink white burgs. this was a major topic of discussion 4-5 years ago when tanzer was bringing it up. But it becomes quite obvious after 1/2-1 hour of air which way a wine is going to go, and if you focus intensely on the wine, it doesn’t really have the hallmarks of true oxidation.

So for a while we all dutifully would leave our oxidized bottles to get several hours of air, even overnight, to be sure we we’re mis-identifiying them. But over time we learned that these bottles exist, but at least for me constitute perhaps constitute at most 5% of the seemingly oxidized bottles. the rest are just pre-moxed and get worse and worse with air.

For me this is an outdated and old topic that most of us have moved beyond. I’m surprised that Jasper is still stuck on it. (I wonder if he is drinking more pristine bottles in terms of provenance and that his curve of the phenomenon that we know of is delayed by several years).

No - That is very clearly not what he is saying at all. Did you listen to the interview? He notes that phenomenon and says that it is rare. Jasper is talking about wines under cork. He is saying that wines under cork go through a phase that mimics oxidation. If you open it while it’s going through that phase then it is dead (most likely - he allows for the rare situation in which it reverses as you describe). But if you wait, and the cork is sound, then the phase mimicking oxidation passes and you get a perfectly aged wine.

For those interested, this all starts at around the 41 minute mark of the podcast. No need to guess at what he is saying. You can just listen.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
If it’s not oxidation, why has adding sulfur (an anti-oxidant) fixed the issue in some domaines? And why have Diam and screwcaps that prevent oxidation prevented the problem? I call bullshit on his theory.

I don’t know any domain where adding sulfur has “fixed” the issue.

Sauzet added sulfur and used a slightly tighter cork. No more issues, previously poster child for premox.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
oh, one more point–why didn’t wines undergo this pseudo-ox phenomenon in the pre-95 winemaking era where pressing was harder, sulfur was higher, batonnage less? And nobody has responded to why screwcap and Diam solve the issue.

Maybe Jasper wants to buy some of my remaining 96s.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
If it’s not oxidation, why has adding sulfur (an anti-oxidant) fixed the issue in some domaines? And why have Diam and screwcaps that prevent oxidation prevented the problem? I call bullshit on his theory.

I don’t know any domain where adding sulfur has “fixed” the issue.

Sauzet added sulfur and used a slightly tighter cork. No more issues, previously poster child for premox.

Nonsense. Lots of incidences of sauzet premox post those changes. Here is a report of an ‘11, which is well after the changes you describe.

WineBerserkers – 20 May 18

TN: 2011 Sauzet Puligny-Montrachet Les Referts - Premox 53 pts

Premox. Slight hint of joy to take it up 3 points from 50 baseline minimum.

And I’d bet the other vintages aren’t far behind. With the exception of Arlot, I can’t think of any producer who can claim that they’ve solved the issue.

link_counts.title: TN: 2011 Sauzet Puligny-Montrachet Les Referts - Premox 53 pts - Wine Berserkers - international wine social media, online community, and discussion

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked: Never mind having solved the problem, too many producers have insisted that their wines haven’t been affected by premature oxidation.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Jasper is no dummy (was said earlier, but worth repeating I think). The fact that premox is still a problem is proof (or an indication) that we have no idea what premox is. And, the recent premox occurrences at Domaine Leflaive seems further proof (they wouldn’t knowingly change things that would increase it, and they’re a well informed domaine)

Also, I don’t know any domaines that solely increased their sulfur (including sauzet) and fixed their premox. Fevre is, so far, the most notable premox turn around…they made a lot of changes, including increased sulfur and changing to a lower oxygen transmission closure (Diam). I’m skeptical that sulfur was the leading (or even notable) factor…I’d guess it’s the closure. Note that Fevre later lowered their sulfur levels some (still higher than they were during the early/mid 2000s).

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Have people had premoxed domestic Chardonnay? I haven’t heard about that. I have heard about premox Beaucastel Blanc. Have people had premix Riesling?

Alex–I was referring/replying to what John had quoted, and got trapped into what I warned folks about above–we can end up talking about two different things.

Yes, I listened to the interview and yes, if I remember correctly, he was referring there to wines that were showing an elevated frequency of pre-mox seeming to be pristine several years later, and that maybe in some cases it could be a temporary phenomenon. I’m really not sure how he could have concluded this, and would assume it was circumstantial evidence–finding frequent “premoxed” bottles in a supply of wine, then leaving it alone for a few years, and subsequently finding none in the same supply, but it would interesting to have him explain himself.

I wonder if anyone else here who has a lot of burg experience has suspected the same phenomenon? And how many believe his suspicions? Not me, at least yet (and I don’t dump or sell my supply of wine if I start getting premox–I keep everything I have and have never sold a bottle. So I have a fair number of leftovers to continue to sample, and no sign yet of a turn around in any heavily affected producer.) I do, however, have the capacity to keep an open mind.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: I haven’t listened to the podcast. John - Is he really espousing a different theory there than he did in the article?

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

You are missing the point. Jasper’s contention is that the wines aren’t oxidized but are temporarily undergoing something that mimics oxidation.

I get it, but have had too many that are exactly like an apple or banana left on counter, they dont get better.

If it’s temporary then its not oxidation, so what is it?

What he should do is coravin and catalog the ones that are “oxidized” and come out of this new phenomenon.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
So if I leave an apple that’s been cut in half and turned brown on tha counter long enough with some sulfur it will become a brand new apple again?

I can’t for the life of me think of how something could become un-oxidized, and would love to know the chemical reaction behind it.

You are missing the point. Jasper’s contention is that the wines aren’t oxidized but are temporarily undergoing something that mimics oxidation.

I get it, but have had too many that are exactly like an apple or banana left on counter, they dont get better.

If it’s temporary then its not oxidation, so what is it?

What he should do is coravin and catalog the ones that are “oxidized” and come out of this new phenomenon.

That’s an interesting idea. I will say that I’ve looked at recent cellartracker notes on bottles from ‘96 that were heavily flawed and I see lots of recent notes for flawed bottles. So at least the data I have doesn’t gibe with what Morris is saying.

At the end of the day, I care very little. I stopped buying white burg after the 2010 vintage and sold everything except the 2010 vintage. I will wait to open my 2010s until the point that they would have been mature. It would be nice to have some support for the idea that I’m doing the right thing, but my guess is that I’ve been fleeced the same way I was with every other white burg vintage of my wine buying lifetime. I’m happy enough with German Riesling, and anyone who still buys white burg for cellaring does so at her own risk.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked: Maybe people should actually listen to his words rather than just commenting without an understanding of the statement. The podcast is free, after all.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
I haven’t listened to the podcast. John - Is he really espousing a different theory there than he did in the article?

I’ve listened once thru prior to all of this discussion, and not re-listened, but what I remember is that yes, he was stating that some wines that seemed to be heavily affected by what might have been premox seemed to be pristine several years later. He didn’t explain how one would be able to conclude this. He speculated, if I remember correctly, that perhaps is was in some cases just a very ugly phase that some wines went thru and came out the other side by some unknown process. I don’t think he was implying that this was all of premox.

Ordinarily, I would regard this as laughable, and just one more example of those with deep relationships with producers trying to give them a break, but I otherwise have respected his judgement. If anyone knows him or has access to him, it would be nice to have him elaborate. As I asked before, if anyone thinks they have noted the same phenomenon, please speak up.

posts.cooked:

If anyone knows him or has access to him, it would be nice to have him elaborate. As I asked before, if anyone thinks they have noted the same phenomenon, please speak up.

You’ve posted extensively in a thread on this very topic

WineBerserkers – 20 Oct 14

Shrinking Premox?

Opened a 2000 Raveneau Vaillons tonight and it’s been an odd experience. My first glass was badly oxidized…Summer and I couldn’t really drink it. Instead of pitching it, I just stuck the bottle into my kitchen’s fridge. About an hour later it seemed...

Does this not remind anyone else of what white Rhone can do? I’ve read extensively about it, although I don’t think I’ve tried enough of it over a period of time to have observed it myself, but I believe this is seen regularly in quality white rhone: at 5-8 years they can appear utterly oxidized, but at around age 15 onwards they are then utterly pristine. Presumably it’s the same process.

Shrinking Premox? - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

Yes, I have! Good work. and I think I’m being entirely consistent. That’s why I warned earlier in this thread that there may be two different issues here and we should be careful which one is being discussed. I’ll go back and re-listen, but my impression was that Jasper wasn’t referring to the wines that perk up with an hour of air, but in fact to wines that we would all regard as appearing to be premoxed.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
I’m very interested in the idea of creating a test for premox that doesn’t involve a Coravin or bottle opening. The bottles are made of glass after all. Could some kind of light/laser be passed through the bottle, with interactions picked up on the other side of the glass? If we can think of ways to study light from stars thousands of light years away, maybe we can figure out a way to study the contents of bottles without affecting them? Chemists, have at it .

There’s something going on with white Burg. We all agree that it’s not well understood. Seems imaginable that what is reported as premox is not one phenomenon, but two (or three or four).

Full disclosure: I don’t buy white Burg for long term storage and consumption. I drink it early.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
How can he prove the bottle was premoxed before?

Some people are just that good.

Marcus - do you know of any one else who does hyperoxidation? It’s supposed to result in a more stable finished wine.

I think oxidation itself can be reversed as a chemical reaction, but I’ve never heard of it with finished wine.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Does this not remind anyone else of what white Rhone can do? I’ve read extensively about it, although I don’t think I’ve tried enough of it over a period of time to have observed it myself, but I believe this is seen regularly in quality white rhone: at 5-8 years they can appear utterly oxidized, but at around age 15 onwards they are then utterly pristine. Presumably it’s the same process.

The example of white rhone is very interesting. But I have never experienced this phenomenon with white burg–ever. Has anyone else?? It’s not as if we haven’t tried.

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked:
I haven’t listened to the podcast. John - Is he really espousing a different theory there than he did in the article?

John, in the podcast my interpretation is he’s talking about a change in the bottle over years. The paragraph from the book does sound more like waiting for a few hours. Possibly that’s just part of the discussion from the book, or maybe something different, I’m not sure. At a dinner last year, we had a conversation with him where he discussed this issue, and he was referencing changes over years from cases where bottles had seemed oxidized in prior years (i.e. what he seems to be saying in the podcast). I remember thinking harder about it afterward than I expected to, and in my mind he’s very worthy of respect. So I’m a bit skeptical but open minded.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
That pseudo-oxidation some wines go through doesn’t include them turning brown. Maybe a little darker golden but not brown. Is Morris saying he’s seen brown bottles lighten back up with more time in the cellar and show well on opening? That would be remarkable.

If he’s repeating the advice from a while back to leave a bottle open a few more hours or overnight, I’m not so sure that’s been a cure for truly oxidized wines. It’s worked for some that I thought might be in very early stages, but I’ve never seen a brown wine rise Phoenix-like the next day.

But if all he’s saying is some bottles show well years after other bottles from the same case showed as premoxed, that just sounds like the randomness of premox. Without documentation of individual bottles going from bad to good, I don’t think he has a convincing story.

Or maybe he’s observing a tendency to be too quick to call premox in the early stages?

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
That pseudo-oxidation some wines go through doesn’t include them turning brown. Maybe a little darker golden but not brown. Is Morris saying he’s seen brown bottles lighten back up with more time in the cellar and show well on opening? That would be remarkable.

If he’s repeating the advice from a while back to leave a bottle open a few more hours or overnight, I’m not so sure that’s been a cure for truly oxidized wines. It’s worked for some that I thought might be in very early stages, but I’ve never seen a brown wine rise Phoenix-like the next day.

But if all he’s saying is some bottles show well years after other bottles from the same case showed as premoxed, that just sounds like the randomness of premox. Without documentation of individual bottles going from bad to good, I don’t think he has a convincing story.

Or maybe he’s observing a tendency to be too quick to call premox in the early stages?

I’ve seen wines that are dark gold and showing oxidation when first opened become lighter in color, more transparent, and less oxidation with exposure to air, most notably some older vintages of Closel Savennieres. I’m not a chemist or physicist, but it seems possible that some of the substances in the wine responsible for its darker and browner appearance could bind with other substances with exposure to air and that the resulting substances reflect and refract light differently than did the original ones. Changes in flavor and aromas could result from similar changes and/or other aromatic substances becoming more prominent with air. Or I could be completely wrong.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Maybe it’s semantics, Mike, or I’m just using a different definition than others but when I’ve had bottles like you’ve described I haven’t thought of them as premoxed.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

That is quite an accusation to make. How do you know they are oxidized? How many old white burgs have you tried? Do you have expertise in evaluating color and the varying shades of glass color for major producers over time? Have you seen the bottles in person?

I have no involvement with these bottles by the way, but this kind of statement does not do much credibility to a BB that wants to be taken seriously.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Does this not remind anyone else of what white Rhone can do? I’ve read extensively about it, although I don’t think I’ve tried enough of it over a period of time to have observed it myself, but I believe this is seen regularly in quality white rhone: at 5-8 years they can appear utterly oxidized, but at around age 15 onwards they are then utterly pristine. Presumably it’s the same process.

This is one of the things that Jasper (Morris) is thinking & referring to. On the first page of this thread, there’s a link to an interesting article about Jasper about this very thing, and various other interesting related things.


Marcus - do you know of any one else who does hyperoxidation? It’s supposed to result in a more stable finished wine.

‘Hyperoxidation’ isn’t that new. I have the first edition of Remington Norman’s Great Domaines of Burgundy, and it talks about hyperoxidation (or perhaps a precursor, they don’t specifically use the hyperO term), esp in the Coche Dury section, where Jean-Francois shrugged his shoulders at the idea (he sulfured the juice immediately to avoid juice oxidation). Anyways, various folks have been working with this for a while.

Note the concept of juice oxidation is to oxidize the ‘easily oxidized’ compounds before alcohol has formed…because the compounds that oxidize in juice (i.e. no alc) aren’t soluble in alcohol. So if you oxidize these compounds as juice, they’ll drop out of solution (precipitate) sometime after fermentation starts (due to the alcohol, of course). If you wait until after fermentation to oxidize these compounds they’ll stay in solution (won’t drop out) and will/might smell up the joint!


I think oxidation itself can be reversed as a chemical reaction, but I’ve never heard of it with finished wine.

Thiols (a compound in wine) can be oxidized (a disulfide, which is an oxidized thiol). Glutathione (an antioxidant compound in wine) can reverse this, turning the disulfide back into a thiol (‘un-oxidizing’ it…as Alan Rath has pointed out, the proper term for this is reduction, but the wine community has stolen this term for another only semi-related purpose, so ‘un-oxidizing’ it is).

This (the Glut ‘un-oxidizing’ the disulfide) happens by ‘rearranging’ things between the Glut and the disulfide, so in the end the Glut is oxidized and the disulfide (oxidized form) has been turned into a thiol (non-oxidized). Basically, the Glut takes one for the team (I’m assuming that an oxidized Glut has little or no sensory impact on the wine…not typically true for most disulfides). Not sure if Glutathione always, or sometimes, does this with disulfides tho. But it’s a plausible oxidation reversing chemical reaction.


The example of white rhone is very interesting. But I have never experienced this phenomenon with white burg–ever. Has anyone else?? It’s not as if we haven’t tried.

I’ve had a number of Dauvissat wines (most often ~10 years old) that initially appeared quite tired and dull when first opened, and for a couple of hours. And then, as if by magic, they transformed themselves into bright/fresh/vibrant/minerally/etc wines. Not sure if this counts or not, but it’s dramatic each time it happens. I’ve also had a 1979 Kistler chardonnay (~25 years old when opened) that was completely dead & oxidized when first opened, and stayed that way for the first ~hour or so…then transformed itself into a relatively youthful interesting wine. Also astonishing to see happen.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Just a speculation: if A->B->C->… as a compound further oxidises from state to state. If an intermediate stage in this chain of oxidation has a significant impact on colour and taste but then further oxidises to a less impacting (color and taste) on the product then we would not need to have a reversal of oxidation to observe the phenomenon…
After all, if you fully oxidise any organic compound containing Carbon and Hydrogen …you end up with CO2 and H2O which are not known as having strong organoleptic or colour…

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
Jasper wasn’t referring to the wines that perk up with an hour of air, but in fact to wines that we would all regard as appearing to be premoxed.

But isn’t that exactly what that 2014 thread is discussing? Wines that people believe to appear as premature oxidation, but getting better with air? I don’t see why Jasper isn’t referring to the same observations shared in that thread

Given that no one has a proven mechanism for how premox normally occurs, I don’t see how we can easily decide that one phenomenon is true premox and another similar phenomenon is not. Or, if there is a consensus definition that includes irreversibility, then it still does not mean the two are unrelated. For all we know, the presentation could very well share the same mechanism.

posts.name: David lo$n
posts.cooked:
I posted this in a previous post but I definitely think it’s relevant.

I will always remember in 2008 I brought a 2005 white burgundy (I believe Bouchard corton) to an outdoor concert.

I opened it and it was terrible; sour and all weird terrible tasting. 1 or two hours later only because I had no where to throw it away at the concert sitting at my blanket I retasted it and it was 100% perfect. I have always rung that up as it was about to completely premox but because it was so new it was able to make a comeback. If it was a year or two older it had no chance.

posts.name: Kevin Kitagawa
posts.cooked:
I’ve seen wines that seemed a bit dumb or flat get better with air…but that’s not premoxed.

I have never (and have tried many times) seen a wine that is actually premoxed come back to life…If you’re getting the heavy caramel or sherry notes on a wine, it is gone.

I would love this to be true…I just doubt it is…

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Marcus - do you know of any one else who does hyperoxidation? It’s supposed to result in a more stable finished wine.

‘Hyperoxidation’ isn’t that new. I have the first edition of Remington Norman’s Great Domaines of Burgundy, and it talks about hyperoxidation (or perhaps a precursor, they don’t specifically use the hyperO term), esp in the Coche Dury section, where Jean-Francois shrugged his shoulders at the idea (he sulfured the juice immediately to avoid juice oxidation). Anyways, various folks have been working with this for a while.

Note the concept of juice oxidation is to oxidize the ‘easily oxidized’ compounds before alcohol has formed…because the compounds that oxidize in juice (i.e. no alc) aren’t soluble in alcohol. So if you oxidize these compounds as juice, they’ll drop out of solution (precipitate) sometime after fermentation starts (due to the alcohol, of course). If you wait until after fermentation to oxidize these compounds they’ll stay in solution (won’t drop out) and will/might smell up the joint!

The issue of (and advantages to) letting the must oxidize came up in a thread last spring on premox in which several of us/you posted. As I understand it, this was a common practice in Burgundy (it tended to occur naturally), then became less common because people fretted when their must turned brown and they begun sulfuring their must. That may, paradoxically, have contributed to oxidative qualities down the road.

Zelma Long and Dave Ramey did a lot of research on it at Simi back in the 80s, and Ramey explains it well in a video interview with Steve Tanzer done for Vinous.

I have always rung that up as it was about to completely premox but because it was so new it was able to make a comeback. If it was a year or two older it had no chance.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Can we at least agree that if the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet is oxidized, it’s not premox?

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
The example of white rhone is very interesting. But I have never experienced this phenomenon with white burg–ever. Has anyone else?? It’s not as if we haven’t tried.

I’ve had a number of Dauvissat wines (most often ~10 years old) that initially appeared quite tired and dull when first opened, and for a couple of hours. And then, as if by magic, they transformed themselves into bright/fresh/vibrant/minerally/etc wines. Not sure if this counts or not, but it’s dramatic each time it happens. I’ve also had a 1979 Kistler chardonnay (~25 years old when opened) that was completely dead & oxidized when first opened, and stayed that way for the first ~hour or so…then transformed itself into a relatively youthful interesting wine. Also astonishing to see happen.
[/quote]

Eric–I’ve had the same experience with multiple Dauvissat wines, which has been the subject of several previous threads. But these wines aren’t oxidized, but are instead reductive. they can come out of the bottle darker, dull, tired, as you says, with some weird odors and not much on the palate. when everyone sees the color, they just assume premox, but I think folks are being sloppy when they call them premoxed. there is no sherry, baked apple, brownish color, that would be typical of premox. These wines then come alive with air and lighten considerably in color, whereas premoxed wines never improve with air. when these reductive wines are included in discussions of actual premox, it seriously clouds the issue and and it gets very hard to really know what anyone is talking about.

Would appreciate to hear Francious Audouze weigh in. I would think he has had many data points and conversations with very experienced community members on wine aging and affects. He has deep experience livening up seemingly oxidized/over the hill wines but what does he think about the prospects for premoxed wines recovering?

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Evidence against this theory is the numerous bottles of dark and oxidized bottles at auction (eg. the 71 Bouchard Chassagne Montrachet and the 91 Leflaive Batard on Winebid right now).

That is quite an accusation to make. How do you know they are oxidized? How many old white burgs have you tried? Do you have expertise in evaluating color and the varying shades of glass color for major producers over time? Have you seen the bottles in person?

I have no involvement with these bottles by the way, but this kind of statement does not do much credibility to a BB that wants to be taken seriously.

Such is the internet. You say a brown bottle of Leflaive is oxidized and someone will argue with you.

I have had 85, 86, 89, and 92 Leflaive which were much much lighter in color so the 91 on Winebid I am guessing is oxided based on my experience. Whether or not it’s dead is another issue and I did not make any comment to that effect for this reason. I doubt many other people would buy that bottle based on the way it looks even if you don’t think it’s oxidized. Cheers.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: I’m no longer sure exactly what people are taking about in this thread but just to reiterate, Jasper’s theory is that the wines aren’t actually oxidized (he talks about an element, but not all of the elements, of the wine being oxidized, or a phenomenon that mimics oxidation). So the suggestion that oxidized wines somehow reverse themselves isn’t part of what Jasper is espousing.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
I’m no longer sure exactly what people are taking about in this thread but just to reiterate, Jasper’s theory is that the wines aren’t actually oxidized (he talks about an element, but not all of the elements, of the wine being oxidized, or a phenomenon that mimics oxidation). So the suggestion that oxidized wines somehow reverse themselves isn’t part of what Jasper is espousing.

That’s a big problem with this type of discussion, and what I tried to warn about early on. To me, a wine that is dark yellow or gold (but not brown), has some weird odors, and not much of a palate, but no true oxidative flavors, and improves significantly with air is not a wine that mimics oxidation. If folks refer to such a wine as one that seems oxidized, it totally confuses the discussion.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
I’m no longer sure exactly what people are taking about in this thread but just to reiterate, Jasper’s theory is that the wines aren’t actually oxidized (he talks about an element, but not all of the elements, of the wine being oxidized, or a phenomenon that mimics oxidation). So the suggestion that oxidized wines somehow reverse themselves isn’t part of what Jasper is espousing.

That’s a big problem with this type of discussion, and what I tried to warn about early on. To me, a wine that is dark yellow or gold (but not brown), has some weird odors, and not much of a palate, but no true oxidative flavors, and improves significantly with air is not a wine that mimics oxidation. If folks refer to such a wine as one that seems oxidized, it totally confuses the discussion.

In addition, I don’t think that folks recognize the difference between pre-moxed and cooked. If you have a young wine with the cork even partially soaked it’s been heat damaged. I think this is a big problem with grey market wines.

I just opened some bottles of 1995 Barthod a few weeks back that have been in my cellar since release and there was only staining on the end of the cork. These were Rosenthal imported, three-tier wines. I think that’s the hidden price of many grey market wines, they are more likely to have seen some sort of transport damage, IMO. I’ve certainly noticed the difference between grey and Rosenthal Barthod. I no longer buy the grey, no matter how good the deal.

Pretty much every single “grey market” retailer for burgundy is using refrigerated reefers across the pond. They all consolidate at Hillebrand in beaune. I’d say the shipping and transportation conditions are as good if not better than most big importers.

There are also really large retailers that also buy outside of the 3 tier system along as within.

These were Rosenthal imported, three-tier wines. I think that’s the hidden price of many grey market wines, they are more likely to have seen some sort of transport damage, IMO. I’ve certainly noticed the difference between grey and Rosenthal Barthod. I no longer buy the grey, no matter how good the deal.

Pretty much every single “grey market” retailer for burgundy is using refrigerated reefers across the pond. They all consolidate at Hillebrand in beaune. I’d say the shipping and transportation conditions are as good if not better than most big importers.

There are also really large retailers that also buy outside of the 3 tier system along as within.

You state that as a fact, but I don’t think you know. Who are the “all” that consolidate with Hillebrand in Beaune? Just because you’ve heard of Hillebrand doesn’t mean anything. Are you ITB now?

Where do you think these wines come from? Do you think Ghislaine is selling to someone cellar door outside of her relationship with Neal? Absolutely not. The way these things work in practice is that brokers and other third parties (some less seemly than others), have lists of wines and folks are buying based on the names of the wines because that’s what there is a market for. Even if a grey marketer had a flawless chain from the time they acquired the wine to the time they put it in your hand there is a big grey area where no one knows what happened to it.

Large retailers do whatever they can do to try to get the labels that sell themselves, however they can manage it. Everyone is welcome to shop how they like, but I’ve had too many negative experiences.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
I can’t prove a negative to you Nathan. Only that the ones I buy from do.

Re: hillebrand

there are plenty of consumers that have their wine purchased in France, Spain, Italy sent to hillebrand to have it imported through an agent.

Feel free to only buy 3 tier. No one is forcing you otherwise.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In addition, I don’t think that folks recognize the difference between pre-moxed and cooked. If you have a young wine with the cork even partially soaked it’s been heat damaged. I think this is a big problem with grey market wines.

While wines do typically taste more advanced stateside than in France, I have had plenty of premoxed bottles in Burgundy, sometimes even on the premises of the domaines with the winemakers.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
The most astonishing case in my experience came during a tasting of several vintages of Domaine Bonneau du Martray’s Corton-Charlemagne. The 1996 was a dark brown on opening and smelled as though it were almost completely oxidized. I fancied there might still be a vestige of fruit on the otherwise oxidized palate, which was a surprise given the dark brown color. But wait… The color after just a few minutes of aeration was now a much paler brown-and 20 minutes later this was the best wine of the evening.

A few years ago I also opened a 96 BdM Corton Charlie which was dark brown and smelled oxidized.
Then it got worse and worse. I even left it on the table overnight, but no change.

hi all
I am currently travelling in New Zealand with not much time available to compose a detailed post, but I will try to flesh something out as soon as I can. Its a really interesting field of discussion (albeit one that has been frequently covered as some posters have noted) and I am not offering any definitive views, but rather discussing experience’s that might lead to further reflections on what the whole premox issue is about, since we remain unable to nail it definitively.
From reading through this thread I think that Alex Gillette has a very good sense of what I am trying to say. I was also interested in the thiols/glutathione commentary in Eric Lundblad’s #65

anyway, will hope to come back with a little more on this when I am back at my desk

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Jasper thanks for participating. While premox is clearly a problem, better understanding is clearly worth pursuing. If for no other reason than the (high) cost of the bottles and the extreme potential for deliciousness if it turns out that some ‘likely to be premoxed’ bottle have some other destiny.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
In addition, I don’t think that folks recognize the difference between pre-moxed and cooked. If you have a young wine with the cork even partially soaked it’s been heat damaged. I think this is a big problem with grey market wines.

While wines do typically taste more advanced stateside than in France, I have had plenty of premoxed bottles in Burgundy, sometimes even on the premises of the domaines with the winemakers.

Sure, I’m not doubting that premature oxidation is a real thing, I’ve experienced enough of it to know first hand. However, I do think there is a lot of confusion about premox vs cooked as they can have the same effect on a wine (and maybe the latter increases the likelihood of the former).

Also, I noticed that you responded to a CT note on Bongran. Have you ever noticed a Thevenet/Goyard/Gillet Macon to be p’oxed? I never have. I’ve had some that were a bit long in the tooth for me, but not oxidized prematurely.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
I can’t prove a negative to you Nathan. Only that the ones I buy from do.

Re: hillebrand

there are plenty of consumers that have their wine purchased in France, Spain, Italy sent to hillebrand to have it imported through an agent.

Feel free to only buy 3 tier. No one is forcing you otherwise.

And you are welcome to do likewise. I think that a lot of folks think that grey marketed wines are just cutting out a layer of the system. They aren’t. For any difficult to acquire wines, they are a work around the official importers and that carries an increased risk even if the retailer is doing everything in their power to prevent it. It is simply the nature of the beast. I’ve found the risk, even with reputable retailers, not worth it for me.

Re: Hillebrand, sure, my FiL uses it all the time. I’m not a balla so it never makes sense for me. I use the handy Wine-check to bring back my small amount of ex-cellar wine.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
If premox is not actually oxidation and can be cleared up over time… then shouldn’t the vast majority clear up then with time or more oxygen instead of small random cases?

Why do some bottles from the same case experience premox when others don’t? This has been debated and speculated on for quite a while and if we had a definitive answer as to what is going on we’d all be a lot happier.

I’ve heard some anecdotal evidence that experience of premox in 2002 Huets is down from what it was a few years ago. Having poured a whole bunch down the sink this news it as annoying as it is hopeful.

Solar flares.

The dreaded blue corks.

Also, I noticed that you responded to a CT note on Bongran. Have you ever noticed a Thevenet/Goyard/Gillet Macon to be p’oxed? I never have. I’ve had some that were a bit long in the tooth for me, but not oxidized prematurely.

Never! But with fermentation in epoxy, long sur lie élevage and high alcohol & residual sugar they exist in their own universe.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
__

what is non-sensical about the argument? I think it’s a little too convenient (if you open a premoxed bottle it’s now your fault for not waiting long enough for the issue to resolve) but is there some reason why what Jasper is saying - which is that there is a temporary condition that mirrors oxidation and then the wine returns to normal - is necessarily untrue?

Not a chemist but I seriously doubt that the reactions leading to premox are reversible. I would need more evidence than one guy’s opinion to be convinced otherwise, no matter how respected he might be.

My guess is that the good bottles were simply the survivors in the case that were never premoxed in the first place.
[/quote]

I doubt it too but Morris is no dummy. And again, the idea is that the wines aren’t actually oxidized and that the effect is later reversed, but suffering from something that temporarily replicates oxidation.
[/quote]

I listened to the tape and I think that’s what he was saying, consistent with his post, above: that this “premox” is a different problem from premature oxidation (though it is unfortunately named to suggest it is the same). He’s found some bottles of '96 have turned out fine, though he thought they were “premoxed”, which seems to convince him that it is a different flaw.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: I will just add that with some of the older bottles I have drunk and found to be pre-moxed not the entire bottle is necessarily darker in color. Often, it seems, after resting so long, there is some sort of oxidation at the bottom that spreads through the wine. Similarly, older bottles that aren’t really premoxed can be somewhat discolored and then when they breath up with air, they lighten up and the offnotes go away. Perhaps this sort of thing is what Jasper and others have experienced.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
It’s just mind boggling that almost 20 years into this problem, no one has a clue. Just theories…myself included. But, the “industry” should be embarrassed. I’m not sure anyone in the industry has really made a sufficient effort to figure it out, especially the cork industry which is the main variable between good and shot bottles.

That Jasper, on tape in 2019, confirms how pathetic the level of understanding is, after all this time.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: So, if the problem is improved with oxidation, then the flaw is some sort of reduction?

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: I’ve been waiting to hear what Jasper has to say before making further comments, but if it turns out that what he’s talking about is this non-premox, weird, probably reductive profile that many of us have experienced, that gets better with air (and perhaps with time in the bottle), I would make a plea that we find a name for it that doesn’t include “premox” in any way–otherwise it becomes way to confusing to discuss. In my mind, if you are careful evaluating such wines, you really don’t find the hallmarks of an oxidized wine, and you can learn to spot them, and set them aside for an hour or two to wake up.

fancy_title: Premox’d White Burgs Turn Great? (per Jasper Morris)
title: Premox'd White Burgs Turn Great? (per Jasper Morris)
